summary: I was born in Mogadishu, Somalia in 1988 and spent my early childhood there until 1997. Somalia is where my roots and culture come from, and I deeply miss the traditional clothes and items I had there. Even though I left as a child, my connection to Somalia remains strong, and I hope to return someday to reclaim and bring back my cultural belongings.

After leaving Somalia in 1997, I lived in a refugee camp in Ethiopia until 2011. Ethiopia was a place of refuge during difficult times, and it shaped much of my early life. It was from Ethiopia that I was authorized for resettlement to the United States in 2009, which changed the course of my life.

I arrived in the United States on December 7, 2011, starting a new chapter with my daughter and sister. The U.S. has given me opportunities for work and education, and though the beginning was challenging, especially losing my suitcase in New York, I have built a life here. The government has played a big role in supporting me, and I am committed to continuing my education and work to improve my future.

When I first landed in New York City in December 2011, I faced the shock of losing one of my suitcases, which held precious photos and cultural items. I was unfamiliar with the city and its transportation, like the train and bus, which confused me at first. Despite this rough start, New York was the gateway to my new life in America.

After New York, I flew to Amarillo, Texas, where I stayed briefly before moving on. Amarillo was part of my early resettlement journey in the U.S., a stepping stone before settling in a more permanent place. It was a new environment that prepared me for the challenges ahead in Texas.

In Cactus, Texas, I worked in a meat plant for about nine months, which was a hard and freezing job standing for eight hours with a knife. It was challenging balancing work and caring for my children, but earning my first paycheck gave me relief and hope. Life in Cactus was tough, and eventually, I decided to move to Minnesota for better opportunities.

I moved to Minneapolis on June 20, 2014, seeking a better life than in Texas. Here, I have access to work and education, and I feel supported by the community and government programs. Minneapolis represents hope and progress for me and my family as we build our future in the United States.
